subject: Phase 2 B.5: KSU injection approach for att-sign native hook inside com.snapchat.android
body: Phase 2 B.3 (JNI scaffold), B.4 (method resolver), and B.5-consumer (SnapCaptureWatcher) are SHIPPED in commits 89a855f / 73bb0a1 / da1f350 / 9d7b1a2 (v0.97.48). The remaining B.5 gap is getting libatt-sign-hook.so RUNNING INSIDE com.snapchat.android. The consumer (SnapCaptureWatcher) is already wired and testable via manual file-drop. This spec documents 3 injection approaches in priority order.

injection_approaches.name: KSU process-monitor service.sh loop (RECOMMENDED)
injection_approaches.description: Write a KSU module whose service.sh starts a background loop that (a) watches for com.snapchat.android process creation via polling pidof or inotifywait on /proc, (b) on detection, uses `su -c 'cat /proc/self/maps | grep att-sign-hook'` to check if already injected, (c) if not, uses KPatch-Next's built-in hook_inject capability if exposed, or nsenter to enter the Snap process mount namespace + inject.
files_needed: A new KSU module zip with module.prop + service.sh + our libatt-sign-hook.so packaged
service.sh: tight loop (sleep 2) checking pidof com.snapchat.android; on new PID, inject
injection_approaches.feasibility: HIGH if KPatch-Next exposes a hook_inject userspace API (check /data/adb/modules/KPatch-Next/bin/kpatch --help for inject subcommand)
injection_approaches.deliverable: tools/sinister-cast/install-att-sign-hook-module.py — builds + pushes the KSU module zip

injection_approaches.name: ptrace injection via root Python daemon
injection_approaches.description: Python daemon (running as root via KSU) that ptrace-attaches to com.snapchat.android, walks its maps to find linker base, injects a dlopen call via register manipulation, then detaches. Standard Android ptrace injection pattern.
challenges: SELinux: ptrace between processes may require setenforce 0 or a custom policy
ARM64 register manipulation is correct but requires careful syscall ABI handling
ART runtime state: must inject between JNI calls, not during GC
injection_approaches.feasibility: MEDIUM — works on non-SELinux-strict KSU devices; may need 'setenforce 0' which is acceptable on our test devices

injection_approaches.name: OverlayFS bind-mount of patched Snap .so
injection_approaches.description: Find which Snap native library contains the AttestationHeadersCallback JNI bridge (likely libargos.so or libargos_jni.so). Binary-patch the entry/exit trampoline for the target method to redirect to our hook. Mount the patched .so over the original via SUSFS overlayfs. Hook is automatic on next Snap launch, no runtime injection needed.
challenges: Requires binary analysis of Snap's native libs (arm64 ELF)
Snap update invalidates the patch (version-specific binary)
Must be careful not to crash Snap's native code
injection_approaches.feasibility: MEDIUM-HIGH for someone with binary patching experience (Luke's domain); LOW for pure Kotlin/Java devs
injection_approaches.deliverable: Cross-lane spec to Luke's kernel/module lane

current_state_of_consumer.SnapCaptureWatcher: SHIPPED — polls /data/adb/sinister/attsign/capture-pending/ every 30s from MainActivity background loop. Reads JSON files, calls AttSignHook.captureFromJson(), moves to processed/failed.
current_state_of_consumer.ATT_SIGN_CAPTURE_broadcast: SHIPPED — SinisterDebugReceiver handles the action. Both ADB broadcast and file-drop paths work TODAY for manual testing.
current_state_of_consumer.att-sign-broadcast.py: SHIPPED — Python ADB broadcast tool for manual capture injection. --help verified.
